text.title: Diary Study Planning
text.summary: Planning steps: defining research questions, making design decisions, building the entry template, and recruiting and onboarding participants.

rule.statement: Define research questions before selecting study type or duration. The questions determine whether a diary study is the right method at all.
rule.rationale: Diary studies answer temporal and contextual questions. Using them for questions better answered by interviews or analytics wastes 2-4 weeks of participant effort.

list.item: How do behaviors change over time?
What is the journey from novice to expert?

section.title: Entry Template Design

rule.statement: Use the five-field entry structure for every study: When (date/time), Where (location/context), What (what happened), How (emotional state), Why (participant's interpretation).
rule.rationale: These five fields capture the complete context needed for temporal and contextual analysis. Omitting "Why" produces entries that describe events without meaning; omitting "Where" prevents contextual pattern analysis.

section.reference: Daily entry template with location picker, satisfaction rating, and open fields.
Event-contingent entry template with trigger, goal, outcome, and optional photo.

rule.statement: Screen participants for willingness to self-document consistently, not just availability. Participants who journal regularly complete studies at 2x the rate of those who do not.
rule.rationale: The biggest predictor of dropout is unfamiliarity with self-documentation. Selection bias from motivated self-reporters is real but acceptable compared to 50% dropout from unscreened participants.

list.item: Representative of target users in goals and context, not just demographics.
Committed for the full duration — screen for this explicitly.
Comfortable with the capture method (app, SMS, paper).
Access to the capture device throughout the study period.

section.title: Onboarding

rule.statement: Conduct a live onboarding session with each participant before the diary period starts. Test their understanding of the entry format with a practice entry.
rule.rationale: Participants who submit a practice entry during onboarding complete 40% more entries than those who only read instructions. The practice entry surfaces confusion before it becomes dropout.

section.reference: Full study plan skeleton covering all planning decisions.
